hints.text: Plates white with **blue letters** (may look plain white through the blur); government vehicles = **green plates**
Gen3 cars: **stubby 3-ridge antenna** car OR **striped white car** (white stripe left, yellow right)
hints.uniq_note: The striped white car is unique to Cambodia (Bangladesh's striped car looks slightly different). The stubby antenna resembles Latin-American cars (MX/CO/EC/BR) — theirs have a diagonally coiled ridge instead of three ridges.

shared_with.note: Bangladesh also has a striped white car, patterned slightly differently.
shared_with.diff: Check Khmer vs Bengali script to confirm.

hints.text: Drives on the **right**

shared_with.diff: Latin script w/ heavy diacritics → Vietnam.
Lao script (Thai-like) → Laos; Khmer is far more intricate.

hints.text: **Khmer script** — very distinct, intricate curvy letters (font-dependent)

shared_with.note: Thai looks similar but less intricate.
shared_with.diff: Left-hand traffic + tiny circles in letters → Thailand.
Simplified look + Lao context (yellow regular plates) → Laos.

hints.text: Poles: concrete **ladder poles** (indents like a ladder) + round concrete poles w/ small holes (rarely wooden)

shared_with.note: Thai poles are **square with pinhole rows** — very uncommon in Cambodia.

hints.text: Common bollards: stone w/ **red-white stripes** or **red top + white base** (reflector in the red part); some are road/region-specific
Road markers: stone, white base + **rounded red top** — road number on the narrow side, town+distance on the main side
Chevrons **black with yellow arrows** (rare; bollards often used instead)
hints.uniq_note: All other SE-Asian countries use the reverse scheme (yellow with black arrows) — black chevrons in SEA mean Cambodia.

hints.text: **Yellow middle lines** (dashed/solid); outer lines rare, white when present; many roads **unpaved**

**Wooden stilt houses** with vertical plank walls, double/triple roofs; walls dark red-brown / light blue / ochre, details painted **bright blue**; tiled orange/red roofs w/ ridge ornaments or metal roofs
Majority **Buddhist** — decorative temples + Buddhist flags (Thailand/Laos temples look similar)
Beer ads everywhere: **Angkor, Anchor, 'Cambodia Beer'**; blue **CPP party signs** (often three men side by side)
**Shitcam** exists (wide circular blur, brownish) — also in India/Bangladesh/Sri Lanka/Nepal; here only in the **southwestern half**
Coverage limited — mostly around **Phnom Penh, Siem Reap, Battambang**
**Central plain** = flat cultivated farmland w/ villages; **northeast** = uncultivated, natural, slightly hilly; **southwest** = hills & low mountains, dense inland forest, populated coast
**White sandy soil** → SE of Phnom Penh + around the city; near the **Thai border**: mountain ridge to the north + red soil (flat roads below the abrupt drop-off)
**Rubber plantations** (thin trunks, taps, neat rows): belt NE of Phnom Penh (Roads 7/71), around Ban Lung + Road 78, around Pech Sreada (dry-season look + very orange soil)

**Bollard spotlights**: black-white bollards ONLY on Road 48 (Tatai↔Road 4 — Thai-style, verify country first!); unique **triangular red-striped bollards** on Road 4 (Angk Snuol↔Preah Sihanouk, esp. after Kirirom), a Ream-NP offroad + Preah Monivong NP

**Road spotlights (SW)**: Road 48→Kiri Sakor under construction (one paved side, red soil); jungle offroads NE of Road 48 (red soil, dense jungle, hilly — eastern lookalikes have browner soil); Phnum Samkos: overcast + big distant mountains, brown unpaved roads, wild green; Road 4 has a **wide red-brown shoulder** (lighter near Phnom Penh); oil palms only on Road 4 north of Preah Nob
Highland plateau: **white rocky soil** + stunted windswept dark-green bushes — very recognisable

**Road spotlights (N/E)**: Road 41 SW of Phnom Penh — wide grey gravelly surface, runs NNE, flat farmland w/ distant hills (angle is key); Roads 6/5 (Siem Reap→Sisophon→Battambang): unique **3-beam poles w/ one-sided hanging insulators** + indented bases (west of Sisophon: insulators both sides); Road 76 south = overcast, dark crisp asphalt, outer lines, jungle hills; Road 76 north (Lumphat↔Mayura) = wide unpaved brown, semi-open jungle, skies clear toward the north
Bilingual signs with **Chinese** writing are common
Massive Khmer temple complex N of Siem Reap — dark weathered stone, extensive **trekker coverage** (huge presence in the official map)
